title: The Needle and the Scale
content:
The needle went in at 9:47 AM.

Jiang Chen counted the seconds because that was what you did when you knew exactly how many you had left. One Mississippi. Two Mississippi. The chemical burn spread up his arm like someone had poured fire into his veins. Three Mississippi. Four. The fluorescent lights above flickered once, twice, and then held steady as if they wanted to watch.

Five Mississippi.

He didn't close his eyes. The priest was still talking, some mumbled prayer about mercy and redemption, but Jiang Chen had stopped believing in those things at fourteen when he'd watched his mother bleed out on a convenience store floor over three hundred yuan. The warden stood behind the glass. The witnesses sat in their chairs, hands folded, faces carefully blank. His lawyer wasn't there. His lawyer had stopped returning calls three weeks ago.

Six Mississippi. Seven.

The burn reached his chest. His heart stuttered once, recovered, stuttered again. He thought about the girl. The one they said he'd killed. Her name was Lin Mei, twenty-three years old, a university student who'd been in the wrong alley at the wrong time with the wrong man standing over her.

Except that man wasn't him.

Eight Mississippi. His fingers started to curl. The muscles in his jaw locked tight. He tried to say something — her name, his name, anything — but his tongue had turned to lead in his mouth.

Nine Mississippi. The lights went white. Then they went black.

And then nothing.

---

Consciousness returned like a knife between the ribs.

Jiang Chen's eyes snapped open. He sucked in a breath that tasted like dust and ozone and something metallic he couldn't name. His hands were still curled. His jaw still ached. But there was no needle in his arm, no gurney beneath him, no ceiling tiles with water stains in the shape of a rabbit.

He was on his knees. On stone. Cold black stone that stretched in every direction like a frozen sea.

Above him, the sky was wrong.

No stars. No clouds. Just a vast, churning darkness that pressed down like a weight, like the whole universe had been turned upside down and he was standing on the bottom of a bowl.

"Jiang Chen."

The voice came from everywhere. Not loud. Not deep. Just present, like the hum of a power line, like the buzz beneath silence.

He forced himself to stand. His legs worked. His arms worked. He was alive — or something close enough to it — and that meant he could fight, could run, could figure out what the hell was happening before he let himself process the fact that he'd just been executed.

The stone under his feet wasn't empty.

Figures stood in the darkness. Tall. Too tall. Their outlines blurred at the edges like heat haze, but their eyes burned with a light that cut through the shadows like blades. He counted them as they stepped forward. One. Two. Five. Ten.

Ten figures, arranged in a semicircle around him. They wore robes of black and red, the fabric shifting in ways that made his eyes ache. Their faces were human — mostly — but wrong in the details. Too many teeth. Eyes that didn't blink. Skin that moved like water over bone.

The one in the center spoke. Her voice was the same hum, the same buzz, but he could see her mouth moving now, forming words that didn't quite match the sounds.

"Jiang Chen. You've been selected for community service."

He laughed.

It came out broken, ragged, the laugh of a man who'd spent three years on death row and learned that the universe had a sick sense of humor. "I'm dead."

"Correct," she said. "And employed."

"I was executed. An hour ago. They put a needle in my arm and — "

"And you passed through the veil," she interrupted. "As expected. You are here because you meet the criteria."

"What criteria?"

She tilted her head. The motion was wrong — too smooth, too fast, like a bird's head on a human neck. "You are dead enough to cross between worlds. You are alive enough to walk among the living. You have no family to miss you. No attachments to hold you back. And you possess skills that are... useful."

"You're saying I got the job because I'm a killer."

"Because you are efficient." She smiled. Her teeth were sharp. "The Ten Courts of Diyu are understaffed. Souls escape. They flee back to the mortal realm, hide among the living, feed on the energy of those who should not yet die. We need someone who can catch them."

Jiang Chen looked down at his hands. They were the same hands that had held a knife at fourteen, that had learned to fight in juvenile detention, that had built a reputation in the criminal underworld of Nanjing before a setup had put him in that chair.

"You need a hunter."

"We need a reaper," she corrected. "A Soul Reaper. Someone who walks the line between death and life. Someone who can touch the dead without becoming them. Someone disposable enough to risk. Valuable enough to keep."

"And if I say no?"

She gestured. The darkness around them rippled, and for a moment he saw what lay beyond the stone floor. Bodies. Thousands of them. Millions. Stretched out in rows that vanished into infinity, their chests rising and falling in a rhythm that didn't match breathing.

"Then you join the queue," she said. "You wait. For judgment. For processing. For the chance to be reborn as a cockroach or a worm or, if the court is feeling generous, a minor bureaucrat in a provincial office for the next three hundred years."

He looked at the bodies. At the endless rows of waiting souls. At the ten figures who stared down at him like a jury that had already made up its mind.

"What's the catch?"

"The souls you hunt are dangerous. Some are old. Some are powerful. Some remember their deaths and want revenge on the living." She paused. "And some are not souls at all. The veil is thin in places. Things cross over that should not. You will handle those as they arise."

"Things."

"Demons. Spirits. Hungry ghosts. The occasional rogue god." She shrugged. "You adapt."

Jiang Chen was quiet for a long moment. He thought about the needle. The burn. The way his heart had stuttered and stopped. He thought about Lin Mei, her face on the news, her mother screaming at the cameras, begging for justice.

He thought about the men who had put him in that chair. The ones who had framed him. The ones who were still breathing, still walking free, while he stood in a dead sky with sharp-toothed judges offering him a job.

"One condition," he said.

The judge raised an eyebrow.

"I want to find out who killed me. Who set me up. And I want them to answer for it."

She considered this. Then she laughed — a sound like breaking glass, like a scream in the distance.

"That is not a condition," she said. "That is a job requirement. All our reapers have unfinished business. It's what makes you useful." She reached into her robe and pulled out something that gleamed in the darkness. A badge. Silver and black, shaped like a scale, with a needle at its center.

"A needle," he said.

"The balance must be maintained." She tossed it to him. It landed in his palm, cold and heavy. "You will learn to use it. You will learn to see what others cannot. You will hunt. You will reap. And when your work is done, you will return here and report to your assigned judge."

"Which one is that?"

She smiled again. "Me. I am Judge Bao, Fifth Court of Diyu. I oversee the souls of the wrongfully condemned." Her eyes burned brighter. "Welcome to the afterlife, Reaper Jiang. You start tonight."

The darkness swallowed her. Swallowed all of them. The stone floor vanished beneath his feet, and he was falling, falling, the badge burning in his hand, the needle pressing against his palm like a promise.

He hit ground.

His eyes snapped open.

He was lying on a rooftop in Nanjing, the city spread out below him like a circuit board, lights flickering in patterns that meant something he couldn't quite read. The air smelled like exhaust and cooking oil and rain. He could hear traffic. Could hear someone shouting in a nearby apartment. Could hear the beat of his own heart, steady and real.

He was back. Alive. Or close enough.

The badge was still in his hand.

He sat up and looked at the city. Somewhere out there, the men who had killed him were still walking. Still breathing. Still thinking they had gotten away with it.

And somewhere out there, a soul had escaped. A hungry ghost. A demon. Something that needed to be caught.

Jiang Chen stood up. He tucked the badge into his pocket. He started walking.

The night was young. And so, apparently, was his second life.

title: The First Soul
content:
Jiang Chen's eyes snapped open.

The ceiling was cracked. Water stains spread across the yellowed plaster like veins. A fly crawled near a bare bulb that buzzed with weak orange light. He smelled mildew. Cigarette smoke. His own sweat.

He was alive.

No—that wasn't right. He was *here*. In a body. Lying on a cot so thin he felt the wooden slats beneath. He sat up fast and his skull slammed into something solid.

"Watch it."

Jiang Chen froze. The voice came from inches away. He turned his head—slowly this time—and found himself staring at a face. A woman. Mid-thirties. Sharp cheekbones. Hair pulled back in a careless knot. Dark eyes that watched him like she was assessing meat at a market.

She sat on a wooden stool beside his cot. A room no bigger than a prison cell. Bare concrete walls. A door with no handle on the inside. A single window painted black.

"Where am I?"

"Your body," she said. "I borrowed it while you were out."

He stared at her. She stared back. Neither blinked.

"You're dead, Jiang Chen. You knew that. But dead doesn't mean gone. Not in this city. Not ever." She pulled a cigarette from behind her ear and lit it with a snap of her fingers. Smoke curled from her nostrils. "I'm Bai Yumo. Your handler. You work for me now."

"I didn't agree to—"

"You did. When you signed your execution papers." She blew smoke at the ceiling. "Article 47 of the Diyu Reintegration Protocol. Condemned prisoners with compatible soul resonance are automatically conscripted into the Soul Reaper Corps upon death. It's in the fine print."

"I was executed by firing squad. There was no fine print."

"There's always fine print." She stood. The stool scraped concrete. She was taller than he expected. Lean. Hard. "Get up. You have work."

Jiang Chen swung his legs off the cot. His body moved. That was the strange part. It moved like he remembered—same weight, same balance, same ache in his left knee from a childhood fall. But something was different. The air felt heavier. The sounds sharper. He could hear the fly's wings. Hear the blood moving in Bai Yumo's veins.

"You're sensing the veil," she said. "It's thinner on this side. You'll get used to it. Most don't."

"Most?"

"Most new Reapers go mad inside three months. The ones who don't learn fast." She walked to the door. It opened before she touched it. Outside was a hallway. Fluorescent lights. Linoleum floor. It looked like every government building in China.

"Where are we?"

"Between. A processing station. We're beneath the city but not in it. Think of it as a subway station for the dead." She started walking. He followed. His bare feet slapped cold linoleum.

"You're going to need shoes."

They passed doors. Hundreds of them. Some were wood. Some were steel. One looked like it was made of human bone. Bai Yumo walked fast, her heels clicking a rhythm that echoed down the empty corridor.

"Your first soul escaped three hours ago. A woman. Name: Li Xia. Age: twenty-nine. Cause of death: suicide by hanging. She was supposed to be processed for reincarnation but she slipped her bindings and crossed back into the mortal world."

"Why?"

"Why what?"

"Why did she escape?"

Bai Yumo stopped walking. She turned. Her eyes were flat. "Doesn't matter. She broke the rules. You bring her back."

"She killed herself. Probably had reasons."

"Everyone has reasons. Reasons don't matter. Only results." She reached into her jacket and pulled out a folded piece of paper. Handed it to him. "Her last known location. She's attached herself to her old apartment. Lingering. Feeding on the living energy of her family. If she stays another three days, she'll start draining them. They'll get sick. Then they'll die."

Jiang Chen unfolded the paper. An address. A photograph. Li Xia looked ordinary. Pretty. A smile that didn't reach her eyes.

"How do I bring her back?"

"You touch her. You speak her true name. And you pull." Bai Yumo demonstrated by grabbing his wrist. Her fingers were cold. "The soul will resist. It will scream. It will try to hurt you. You don't let go. You don't listen. You bring it back to the processing station and hand it over."

"That's it? Touch, name, pull?"

"That's it. Simple. Hard to do." She released him. "One more thing. Souls that escape and feed become stronger. Hungrier. They learn to hide. They learn to lie. Li Xia has been out for six hours. She's already stronger than when she left."

"How much stronger?"

Bai Yumo didn't answer. She turned and walked away. Her voice floated back over her shoulder. "Find out yourself. You're the Reaper now."

---

The door to the mortal world looked like a service entrance. Gray steel. A push bar. A sign that read EMERGENCY EXIT ONLY — ALARM WILL SOUND.

Jiang Chen pushed through.

He emerged in an alley behind a restaurant. Steam rose from a vent. Grease slicked the asphalt. A cat hissed and vanished into shadows. The air smelled like fried garlic and garbage. Normal. Alive.

He was wearing the clothes Bai Yumo had given him. Black pants. Black jacket. Cheap sneakers. A uniform. He looked like a delivery driver.

The address was twenty minutes away on foot. He walked. The city felt different now. The streetlights hummed at frequencies he'd never noticed. The people passing by left trails of heat and sound—heartbeats, breathing, the electrical crackle of their nervous systems. He saw a man on his phone and heard both sides of the conversation. He saw a woman crying on a bench and smelled the salt of her tears before he was close enough to see them.

The veil was thin. And he was on the wrong side.

Li Xia's apartment building was old. Pre-war. Red brick with peeling paint and a courtyard full of rusted bicycles. The front door was unlocked. He climbed three flights of stairs. The hallway was dark. A single bulb flickered at the far end.

He found her door. Apartment 304. A wreath hung on it. Dried flowers. A photo of Li Xia taped to the center. Her family had made a memorial.

He knocked.

No answer.

He tried the handle. Locked. He put his hand on the door and pushed—not hard, just pressed his palm flat against the wood. The lock clicked. The door swung open.

The apartment was dark. Curtains drawn. The air was still and cold, colder than it should be. He stepped inside.

"Li Xia."

Silence.

"I'm not here to hurt you. I'm here to take you home."

A whisper. From the bedroom. He walked toward it. The floorboards creaked beneath his feet. The cold deepened with every step. His breath fogged in front of him.

He pushed the bedroom door open.

She was in the corner. Curled up. A woman in a white dress, her back to him, her shoulders shaking. She was crying.

"Go away."

"I can't."

"I didn't mean to die." Her voice cracked. "I just wanted it to stop. The pain. The noise. I wanted it to stop and now I can't go back and I can't move forward and I'm stuck here watching them cry over me."

She turned.

Her face was the photograph. Same eyes. Same mouth. But her skin was wrong. Pale. Translucent. Veins visible beneath like rivers under ice. Her eyes were black. Not the iris—the whole eye. Two pools of darkness that drank the light.

"You're dead like me," she said. "Why do you get to walk? Why do you get to breathe?"

"I didn't choose this."

"Neither did I." She stood. Her bare feet didn't touch the floor. She floated inches above it. "I hung myself in that bathroom. I felt the rope tighten. I felt my neck break. I felt myself die piece by piece and no one came. No one saved me."

"Li Xia—"

"My name." Her face twisted. "You think you can use my name to bind me? I heard you coming. I felt you walking through the city like a bell. You're not a Reaper. You're a corpse wearing a uniform."

She lunged.

Fast. Faster than anything alive. Her hands closed around his throat and he hit the wall so hard the plaster cracked. Her grip was iron. Her fingers dug into his flesh and he couldn't breathe.

"Die again," she hissed. "Die again and stay dead."

He grabbed her wrists. Her skin was freezing. Burning. He tried to pull her hands away but she was too strong. His vision blurred. Spots danced at the edges.

*Say her name.*

He tried. No air. No sound.

*Say it.*

He opened his mouth. Forced a whisper. "Li—"

She squeezed harder.

"—Xia."

Something broke.

Not his neck. The air. The space between them. Her grip loosened. Her eyes widened. She started to scream—a sound that wasn't human, wasn't alive, a sound that came from somewhere deep and ancient.

He grabbed her arm. Pulled.

She came apart.

Not like flesh. Like smoke. Like water. Her body dissolved into threads of white light that wrapped around his hand and pulled toward his chest. He felt her enter him. Felt her terror and her rage and her grief flood through his veins like poison.

He fell to his knees.

The apartment was empty. The cold was gone. The curtains fluttered in a breeze from an open window.

He looked down at his hands. They were shaking. And on his palm, written in light that faded even as he watched, was a name.

Not Li Xia's.

His own.

*Jiang Chen: Wrongfully executed. Case reopened.*

The writing vanished. He stared at his empty palm. His heart hammered.

Bai Yumo had said the souls would try to hurt him. She hadn't said they'd give him answers.

He stood on shaky legs. Walked to the bathroom. Opened the door.

A woman's body still hung there. Rope around neck. Face purple. Eyes open.

Li Xia's body. Her real body. The one her family had buried three days ago.

Except someone had dug it up and hung it again.

Someone wanted him to find this.

The door to the apartment swung shut behind him. Locked. From the outside.

Jiang Chen turned. Through the wood, he heard footsteps. Slow. Deliberate. Walking away.

And then a voice. Male. Familiar.

"Welcome to the job, Reaper. Your first murder case starts now."

title: The Second Soul on the List
content:
The hallway stretched longer than any corridor in any prison Jiang Chen had ever walked.

He counted his steps. Forty-seven. Forty-eight. The fluorescent lights flickered overhead, casting the white floor tiles in sickly yellow. Each door he passed looked identical — steel frame, small window, number plaque. But the numbers didn't make sense. 217. 31. 8,842. The underworld had its own mathematics.

The paper in his hand listed two names. He'd caught the first one — the woman with the burned face — and watched her dissolve into the judge's ledger like ink into water. The process had taken seconds. Her suffering had taken years.

Now the second name stared up at him.

*Chen Wei. Male. 34. Cause of death: heart failure.*
*Stolen years: 22.*

Twenty-two years of someone else's life. That meant whoever took Chen Wei's remaining time was still walking around, breathing borrowed air, feeling borrowed heartbeats.

Judge Zhao's words echoed in his skull. *"The soul usually returns to the place where it died. Or the place it loved most."*

Chen Wei's file said he'd collapsed at a construction site in the eastern district. Scaffolding collapse. Three workers died that day. Chen Wei was the only one whose soul never crossed over.

Jiang Chen stopped at the end of the hallway. A metal door. No handle. No window. Just a solid sheet of rusted iron.

He touched the surface. Cold. Alive in a way metal shouldn't be.

"You'll need to cut yourself," said a voice behind him.

He spun.

The woman stood three feet away. He hadn't heard her approach. She wore the same gray robes as the other clerks, but hers were stained with something dark along the cuffs. Ink, maybe. Or blood. Her face was plain, unremarkable — the kind of face you'd forget five seconds after looking away.

"Excuse me?"

"The door." She pointed at the rusted surface. "It's sealed with blood magic. Your blood will open it. A living soul's blood carries the resonance of the mortal realm. The door needs that to let you back through."

Jiang Chen looked at the door. Then back at her. "You're not going to tell me how you know that?"

"I'm a clerk in the Department of Records. I know a lot of things." She smiled. It didn't reach her eyes. "Name's Li Mei. I'm your handler."

"I don't need a handler."

"Judge Zhao thinks you do." She pulled a small notebook from her sleeve. "First assignment. Two souls. You caught one. But the second one is trickier. Chen Wei escaped capture because he didn't cross over properly. He's still attached to his body."

"His body's buried."

"His body's in a morgue. The family refused cremation. They're waiting for the investigation to close." She flipped a page. "The construction company is being sued. Three families. Chen Wei's wife is the lead plaintiff."

Jiang Chen stared at her. "How do you know all this?"

"I told you. Records." She tucked the notebook away. "Cut yourself. Go through. Catch the soul. Come back. Try not to die again — the paperwork is a nightmare."

She turned and walked away, her footsteps silent against the tiles.

Jiang Chen watched her go. Something about her bothered him. She was too casual. Too familiar. Like she'd done this before. Like she'd handled other Soul Reapers.

He turned back to the door. Pulled the small dagger from his belt — standard issue, the clerk had called it. One cut. Quick and shallow. Blood welled up on his palm.

He pressed his hand against the rusted iron.

The metal drank his blood. He felt it pull, like a mouth sucking at a wound. The rust began to glow. Red, then orange, then the color of dying embers.

The door swung open.

On the other side: a street. Nighttime. Rain falling. The familiar smell of exhaust and wet concrete.

Shanghai.

He stepped through.

---

The construction site was a skeleton of steel beams and concrete floors. Yellow caution tape fluttered in the wind. A half-finished tower loomed against the gray sky, its upper floors lost in the clouds.

Jiang Chen stood at the base, rain dripping down his collar. He'd been standing there for ten minutes, watching. Waiting.

The soul should be here. Chen Wei died here. His unfinished business was here.

But the site was empty.

No workers. No ghosts. No flickering shapes at the edge of vision.

Something was wrong.

He pulled out the paper the judge had given him. The ink shifted as he watched, rearranging into new characters.

*Chen Wei. Not at death site. Location: unknown.*

"Unknown?" He crumpled the paper. "What kind of tracking system is this?"

The rain picked up. Thunder rolled across the sky.

He needed to think. Judge Zhao had said souls usually return to places of death or love. Chen Wei loved his wife. His wife was at the morgue, fighting for justice.

If Chen Wei's soul was still attached to his body, he wouldn't go far from it.

Jiang Chen started walking.

---

The Shanghai Public Morgue was a squat concrete building tucked between two high-rises. No signage. No windows. Just a steel door and a security camera.

Jiang Chen approached the door. It was locked. Electronic keypad.

He touched the keypad. The screen flickered. The numbers rearranged themselves. A soft click.

The door swung open.

He didn't question it. The underworld had its own rules. Its own keys.

Inside, the morgue was cold. Sterile. The kind of cold that settled into bones and stayed there. Fluorescent lights buzzed overhead. The hallway was empty.

He walked past rows of refrigerated drawers. Each one held a body. Each one held a story cut short.

He found Chen Wei's drawer at the end. Number 47.

He pulled it open.

The body inside was pale. Waxy. The face of a man who'd died surprised — mouth slightly open, eyes closed but not fully, like he'd been interrupted mid-sentence.

Jiang Chen looked at the body. Then he looked closer.

There were marks on the chest. Bruises. Fingertip-shaped bruises. Like someone had grabbed him hard enough to leave impressions on the bone.

The file said heart failure. But these bruises were fresh. They'd been made at the moment of death.

Someone had held Chen Wei down. Held him still. While he died.

"You're not here for the body."

The voice came from behind him.

He turned.

The man standing in the doorway was tall. Broad-shouldered. Wearing a police uniform. His face was familiar in the way nightmares are familiar — you've never seen it before, but your body remembers the fear.

"I asked you a question," the officer said. "What are you doing here?"

Jiang Chen didn't answer. He was looking at the officer's chest. At the faint glow coming through the fabric.

A soul. Bright. Wrapped tight around the officer's heart.

Chen Wei's stolen years.

The officer followed his gaze. Looked down. Saw the glow.

His face went hard. "You can see it."

"I can see a lot of things," Jiang Chen said. "Like the fact that you're not a cop. You're wearing a stolen uniform. Stolen badge. Stolen life."

The officer's hand moved to his belt. Toward his gun.

"You don't want to do that," Jiang Chen said.

"You don't know what I want." The officer's eyes had changed. They were dark. Hollow. The eyes of a man who'd done terrible things and planned to do more. "But I know what you are. You're one of them. One of the judges' dogs."

Jiang Chen's hand went to his dagger. "Then you know I'm not here to negotiate."

"I know." The officer drew his gun. "But I also know you're not supposed to kill the living. Rule number one, isn't it? Don't interfere with the living."

He aimed the gun at Jiang Chen's chest.

"You might be dead already," the officer said. "But I wonder how many bullets it takes to stop a corpse?"

The gunshot was loud. Deafening in the small room.

Jiang Chen felt the bullet hit his chest. Felt it punch through skin and muscle. Felt it lodge somewhere near his spine.

He didn't fall.

He looked down at the wound. Blood, but not much. The bullet had passed through him like he was made of paper.

The officer's eyes went wide.

Jiang Chen pulled the dagger from his belt. "Rule number two," he said. "Don't threaten a Soul Reaper in a room full of dead bodies."

He moved forward.

The officer fired again. Two shots. Three. Each one hit, each one passed through.

Jiang Chen didn't slow down. He grabbed the officer by the throat. Slammed him against the wall.

"Where did you get the stolen years?" His voice was calm. Too calm. "Who sold them to you?"

The officer laughed. Blood bubbled at his lips. "You think I'm the buyer? You think I'm the one at the top?" He laughed harder. "I'm just a customer. Just a man who wanted more time."

"Who sold them to you?"

"Find the merchant," the officer said. "Find the soul merchant. He's the one who cuts the years from dying men. He's the one who sells them to people like me."

"Where do I find him?"

The officer's eyes met his. "He finds you."

And then the light in his chest went out.

The stolen years detached. They floated up, a golden mist that swirled around Jiang Chen's hand. He grabbed them. Tight.

The officer's body went limp.

He'd killed a living man.

He'd broken the rule.

Jiang Chen let the body fall. He looked at the mist in his hand. Chen Wei's stolen years. Twenty-two of them.

But the soul was still missing.

And now he had a name for the enemy.

The soul merchant.

He turned to leave. The mist followed him, clinging to his fingers like smoke.

At the door, he stopped.

The officer's body was still warm. Still bleeding. Still alive in the way fresh corpses are alive.

But in the corner of the room, a shadow moved.

Jiang Chen turned.

Chen Wei's soul stood in the corner. Transparent. Flickering. His eyes locked on the body of the man who'd stolen his years.

"Thank you," the soul whispered.

"Don't thank me yet," Jiang Chen said. "I still have to bring you back."

The soul smiled. It was a sad smile.

"I'm not going back."

Jiang Chen frowned. "What do you mean?"

"I mean I've seen what's on the other side." Chen Wei's voice was hollow. "I've seen the ledgers. I've seen what happens to souls who owe debts."

"What debts?"

The soul pointed at Jiang Chen's chest. At the mark that only the dead could see.

"You're carrying a debt too," Chen Wei said. "You just don't know it yet."

The lights flickered. The room went dark.

When they came back on, Chen Wei was gone.

And Jiang Chen was alone with a dead body and a name that burned like acid in his mind.

The soul merchant.

He had to find him.

But first, he had to figure out why every soul he touched seemed to know something he didn't.

He looked down at his hands. The mist was still there. Golden. Warm. Alive.

Twenty-two years of someone else's life.

And

title: The Truth Beneath the Skin
content:
The rooftop air tasted like exhaust and rain. Jiang Chen sat cross-legged on the concrete edge, the Yan Huang Scripture open in his lap, though the words had stopped making sense an hour ago.

Below, the city hummed with its usual night traffic. Taxis. Late-night delivery bikes. A couple arguing outside a convenience store. Normal sounds. Living sounds.

He wasn't living.

The thought settled in his chest like a stone. He pressed a hand to his ribs, feeling for a heartbeat that wasn't there. Cold. Still. The bullet wound had healed into a pale scar, but the hole in his chest felt wider than any bullet could make.

Three days. Three days since Judge Zhang had pressed that seal into his hand and told him he was a Soul Reaper now. Three days since he'd hunted his first ghost — a woman named Lin Mei who'd been haunting her own funeral, confused and terrified, not knowing she was dead.

He'd guided her to the Yellow Springs. She'd cried. He'd felt nothing.

The Yan Huang Scripture said Soul Reapers were supposed to be impartial. A tool of cosmic law, nothing more. But the words on the page felt like excuses, not explanations.

His phone buzzed. A text from Old Zhao: *Got something. Warehouse District. East of Third Bridge. Come alone.*

Jiang Chen stood. The scripture folded itself into his coat pocket like it had a mind of its own. He'd learned not to question the small magics anymore.

The warehouse was a rusted skeleton against the night sky, its windows boarded, its doors chained. But the chains were new. And the lock was polished brass — expensive, out of place.

Jiang Chen circled to the back. A window on the second floor had been pried open, the wood splintered. He climbed, his body moving with a lightness that still surprised him. The dead had advantages.

Inside, the air smelled of mold and metal. Old machinery sat under tarps, a graveyard of forgotten industry. But someone had been here recently. A single light bulb hung from the ceiling, casting a yellow circle on the concrete floor.

And in that circle stood a man.

He was tall. Chinese, maybe forty, with the build of someone who'd spent years not needing to fight but knowing how. His suit was expensive — tailored, dark gray, no wrinkles. His hands were in his pockets, and he was smiling.

"You're harder to find than I expected," the man said. His voice was smooth. Confident. The voice of someone used to being in control.

Jiang Chen stayed in the shadows. "Who are you?"

"Someone who knows what you are." The man stepped closer. The light caught his face — sharp cheekbones, eyes that missed nothing. "I know you died three weeks ago. I know you came back. I know you work for the dead now."

The words hit like a physical blow. Jiang Chen felt his fingers curl into fists. "That's not possible."

"Nothing is impossible, Mr. Jiang. You should know that better than most." The man reached into his jacket. Jiang Chen tensed, ready to move, but the man only pulled out a photograph.

He tossed it. It landed at Jiang Chen's feet, face up.

The photograph showed a courtroom. Not the execution chamber — the trial. Jiang Chen sat at the defendant's table, his head bowed, his hands cuffed. The prosecutor stood at the podium, pointing at him with a finger like a blade.

The prosecutor was the man in front of him.

"You," Jiang Chen whispered. The word came out wrong. Broken.

"Me." The man smiled wider. "Prosecutor Liang Wei. I put you on death row, Mr. Jiang. I made sure the evidence was clean, the witnesses were credible, and the judge had no choice but to sentence you to execution."

"Why?"

"Because you were innocent."

The silence that followed was absolute. The warehouse held its breath. Jiang Chen felt the floor tilt beneath him, felt the world reorder itself around a truth he'd always known but never been able to prove.

"I was framed," he said. Not a question.

"You were framed beautifully. The murder weapon had your fingerprints. The security footage showed someone who looked exactly like you entering the victim's apartment. The neighbor identified you in a lineup. It was perfect." Liang Wei's smile didn't waver. "The only problem was that you were in a jail cell two miles away when the murder happened."

Jiang Chen's hands were shaking. He couldn't stop them. "Then why did they convict me?"

"Because the real killer was someone important. Someone whose name couldn't appear in the investigation." Liang Wei took another step closer. "Do you want to know who?"

The rage was a living thing inside him, clawing at his ribs, screaming to get out. But something held it back. Something cold and careful, the part of him that had learned to survive three years on death row.

"If you know all this," Jiang Chen said slowly, "why are you telling me now?"

Liang Wei's smile faded. For the first time, something real flickered in his eyes. Something that looked almost like fear.

"Because she's coming for me next."

"She?"

"The woman who ordered your execution. The woman who's been killing people for twenty years and framing innocent men for every death." Liang Wei's voice dropped. "She's a judge, Mr. Jiang. One of the Ten Judges of Diyu."

The words didn't make sense. They couldn't make sense. "The Ten Judges are dead. They're spirits. They don't—"

"They don't what? They don't walk among the living?" Liang Wei laughed, but there was no humor in it. "You're a dead man walking the earth, Mr. Jiang. You hunt ghosts. You carry a scripture that shouldn't exist. And you think a Judge can't put on a mortal face and walk through a courtroom?"

Jiang Chen's mind raced. Judge Zhang. The old man in the robe, his face weathered by centuries. He'd seemed ancient, otherworldly. But he'd also seemed tired. Bureaucratic. Like a man doing a job he'd been doing for too long.

Could a Judge be evil? Could a Judge kill?

"You're lying," Jiang Chen said. But his voice wavered.

"I wish I was." Liang Wei reached into his jacket again. This time, he pulled out a file. Thick. Dog-eared. "This is every case she's touched. Every innocent man she's sent to death row. Every family she's destroyed. I've been building this for ten years, Mr. Jiang. And three weeks ago, she found out."

The file landed at Jiang Chen's feet beside the photograph.

"She's going to kill me. Not through the courts — through her power as a Judge. She'll send something after me. Something that makes my death look like an accident, or a heart attack, or a suicide." Liang Wei's hands were shaking now. "I need protection. And you're the only person in the world who can give it to me."

"Why would I help you? You put me on death row."

"Because I'm the only person who can prove you were innocent." Liang Wei met his eyes. "Help me survive, and I'll give you the evidence. You'll have your name back. You'll have your life back."

The words hung in the air. Jiang Chen looked down at the file, at the photograph, at the truth lying at his feet.

He thought about his mother, crying in the visitor's room. He thought about the guards, the needles, the cold table. He thought about Judge Zhang, about the Yan Huang Scripture, about the seal that marked him as property of the dead.

And he thought about the woman who had ordered all of it.

"I'll help you," Jiang Chen said. "But not for my name. Not for my life."

Liang Wei frowned. "Then why?"

"Because I want to know her name. I want to know her face. And I want to be the last thing she sees before she dies."

The words came out flat. Cold. The voice of a dead man who had nothing left to lose.

Liang Wei stared at him for a long moment. Then he nodded.

"Her name is Judge Zhang," he said. "The Judge of the Fifth Court. The one who recruited you."

The world stopped.

Jiang Chen's hand went to his chest, to the seal hidden beneath his shirt. The seal that Judge Zhang had pressed into his skin. The seal that bound him to the underworld.

The seal that had been placed there by the woman who had murdered him.

"No," he whispered. "That's not possible."

"She walks among the living as a mortal woman," Liang Wei said. "Gray hair. Old face. But her soul is ancient. I've seen her true form once, in a vision — she's not what she appears to be."

The warehouse door slammed open.

Both men turned. A figure stood in the doorway, silhouetted against the streetlights. Small. Bent. An old woman in a traditional robe.

Judge Zhang.

"Jiang Chen," she said, her voice carrying the weight of centuries. "Step away from the prosecutor. He's not what he appears to be either."

Jiang Chen looked at Liang Wei. The prosecutor's face had gone pale. His hand was reaching for something inside his jacket.

"Don't trust her," Liang Wei said. "She'll twist everything. She'll make you believe—"

"She already made me believe," Jiang Chen said. "She made me believe I was dead. She made me believe I was a Soul Reaper. She made me believe I was serving justice."

He turned to face Judge Zhang. The old woman stood in the doorway, her hands clasped in front of her, her eyes unreadable.

"I want the truth," Jiang Chen said. "Both of you. Right now. Or I walk away and let the dead sort it out."

The silence stretched. The light bulb flickered.

Judge Zhang spoke first.

"The prosecutor is a ghost," she said. "He's been dead for six months. And he's been lying to you since the moment you arrived."

Liang Wei's face twisted. His hand came out of his jacket, holding a blade that glowed with pale light.

"I'm not the ghost here," he said. "She is. She's been dead for three thousand years. And she's been using you, Jiang Chen. Just like she's used everyone."

Jiang Chen looked between them. The old woman. The prosecutor. The photograph. The file. The scripture in his pocket, whispering secrets he couldn't understand.

Two liars. One truth.

And the only person who could find it was a dead man holding a blade that could cut through lies.

The light bulb went out.

In the darkness, Jiang Chen heard the sound of a blade being drawn.

And then someone screamed.

title: The Debt Collector's Ledger
content:
The old woman's soul tasted of rust and regret.

Jiang Chen pulled his spectral senses back, letting the flavor fade from his tongue. He stood in the pouring rain outside a noodle shop in Chengdu's old quarter, watching steam rise from the street drains. Three weeks since his execution. Three weeks of hunting.

Three weeks of learning what ghosts tasted like.

"The target's name is Zhao Guifen," Chen Lu said beside him, her voice flat as a blade. She held an umbrella but the rain still found her shoulders. "Died 2003. Lung cancer. Refused to cross over. Started possessing mediums three years ago."

Jiang Chen wiped rain from his face. "What's she doing now?"

"What she always does. Finds a grieving family, offers to speak to their dead relatives, takes their money. Last month she convinced a widow to sell her apartment."

The street was quiet for a Friday night. Red lanterns hung wet and dim above the noodle shop. Through the window, Jiang Chen could see a woman in her sixties sitting alone at a table, hands wrapped around a bowl of soup she wasn't eating.

"Her daughter died," Chen Lu said, following his gaze. "Car accident. Zhao Guifen's been contacting her for the past week. Charging five hundred yuan per session."

Jiang Chen watched the woman's shoulders shake. She was crying into untouched noodles.

"Can't the family tell it's fake?"

"Grief makes people stupid." Chen Lu's tone carried no judgment. Just fact. "Zhao Guifen knows exactly what to say. She reads their memories while possessing the medium, pulls out details only the dead would know. By the time the family realizes they've been conned, she's already moved cities."

"And we just drag her back to Diyu."

"Correct."

Jiang Chen had done this four times now. Each soul had a different story. A teenage boy who killed himself and regretted it instantly. A businessman who faked his death and tried to restart his life in Thailand. A grandmother who just wanted to see her grandson graduate.

They'd all fought. They'd all lost.

But Zhao Guifen was different. She was still in her original body, preserved by a combination of dark arts and sheer stubbornness. Her soul had never crossed the veil, never seen the judges, never received her sentence. She'd been running for seventeen years.

"You're thinking something," Chen Lu said.

"I'm wondering why the Ten Judges haven't sent stronger reapers after her."

She turned to face him fully. The rain had plastered her black hair to her skull, her umbrella long abandoned. "What makes you think they haven't?"

Jiang Chen felt the weight of that statement settle in his chest. "How many?"

"Three. All qualified. All experienced." She paused. "She killed two. The third quit and refused to leave the mortal realm."

"Then why send me?"

Chen Lu's silence was answer enough.

He was bait. A fresh soul reaper with barely enough training to hold his staff. Zhao Guifen would underestimate him, get sloppy, and then—

"Don't take it personally," Chen Lu said. "It's efficient."

"Efficient." Jiang Chen watched the old con woman through the window. She'd finished her performance. The grieving widow was handing over an envelope of cash. "What happens if I fail?"

"You don't."

She said it like she believed it. Jiang Chen wasn't sure he shared her confidence.

---

The medium's apartment was on the fourth floor of a building with no elevator. The stairwell smelled of cooking oil and damp laundry. Zhao Guifen had rented it under a false name, paid in cash, left no trail.

Jiang Chen climbed the stairs alone. Chen Lu was watching the exits outside.

The door was cheap wood with a flimsy lock. He could have kicked it in. Instead, he knocked.

A long pause. Then a woman's voice, old and tired: "Who is it?"

"Building maintenance. There's a gas leak reported on this floor."

Another pause. He heard footsteps, the sound of a chain being slid aside. The door cracked open.

The medium was a woman in her forties with hollow cheeks and haunted eyes. Zhao Guifen's soul wore her like a coat. Jiang Chen could see the old woman's spirit flickering behind the medium's gaze, watching him with cold calculation.

"There's no gas leak," Zhao Guifen said.

"No," he agreed. "But you wouldn't have opened the door otherwise."

He pushed. The medium stumbled back. Jiang Chen stepped inside and closed the door behind him.

The apartment was sparse. A table, two chairs, a bed in the corner. Photos of strangers on the walls — families Zhao Guifen had conned, houses she'd visited. Souvenirs of grief.

"You're young," Zhao Guifen said. The medium's body was tense, ready to fight or flee. "Younger than the others."

"I'm new."

"They send the new ones to die."

"Apparently."

She studied him. The medium's face shifted, muscles moving independently of Zhao Guifen's control. Then the old woman's soul stepped forward, pushing the medium aside. The body's eyes changed — deeper, older, more cunning.

"You're already dead," she said. "I can smell it. Like rotting flowers."

Jiang Chen pulled out his soul reaper's staff. It was black iron, cold in his hands, inscribed with characters that glowed when he channeled power through them. "I'm here to bring you back to Diyu for judgment."

"Judgment." Zhao Guifen laughed. It was a terrible sound. "You want to talk about judgment? I was a good woman in life. I raised three children. I never stole. I never lied. And then I got cancer. The doctors said I had six months. I lasted eight, in agony, and when I finally died, do you know what the judges told me?"

Jiang Chen didn't respond.

"They told me I'd done enough good deeds to avoid hell, but not enough to earn rebirth. They said I'd have to wait. Wait in Diyu for a thousand years before I could try again." Her voice cracked. "A thousand years. For what? For being born poor? For not being virtuous enough?"

"So you ran."

"So I ran." She stepped closer. The medium's body moved with an unnatural grace, bones shifting beneath skin. "I found this woman. She was already dying. I pushed her soul aside and took her body like putting on a coat. And I've been living ever since."

"That's not living. That's stealing."

"Living is all I have." Her eyes burned. "I've been seventeen years in this body. Seventeen years of sunrises. Seventeen years of tasting food and feeling rain on my skin. Do you know what I'd give to keep that?"

"Your soul."

"Cheap price."

Jiang Chen tightened his grip on his staff. "Last warning. Come willingly, or I'll drag you back."

Zhao Guifen smiled. It was the smile of someone who had nothing left to lose. "I killed two of your kind. Both stronger than you. Both faster." She reached into her sleeve and pulled out a talisman — red paper, black ink, characters that seemed to writhe. "What makes you think you're different?"

She threw the talisman. It exploded into a cloud of black smoke that burned his eyes and filled his lungs. Jiang Chen coughed, stumbled back, felt the smoke seep into his soul.

Dark arts. She'd learned dark arts.

Through the smoke, he saw her coming. The medium's body was a blur, nails extended into claws, mouth open in a silent scream. She was possessed now — fully, completely. The medium's soul had been consumed.

Jiang Chen raised his staff and took the blow.

The impact sent him through the wall.

He crashed into the next apartment, debris raining around him. An elderly couple screamed from their bed. Jiang Chen scrambled to his feet, apologized to them through a mouthful of dust, and jumped back through the hole.

Zhao Guifen was waiting.

Her claws raked across his chest. Pain exploded through him — not physical, but deeper. She was tearing at his soul, trying to unravel him from the inside. He felt threads of himself coming loose.

He grabbed her wrist. She was stronger than him. Much stronger.

"You're dying," she whispered. "Again. And this time there's no reincarnation. This time you'll just stop existing."

Jiang Chen looked into her eyes and saw the years of running. The fear. The hunger. She was a cornered animal, willing to do anything to survive.

He understood her.

That was his mistake.

She saw it in his face — the flicker of empathy — and she used it. Her soul surged forward, trying to possess him. He felt her pressing against his defenses, looking for cracks, looking for weakness.

And she found one.

His death.

She saw it. The execution. The needle. The cold table. The witnesses. She saw it all in a flash, and she stopped.

"Your death," she breathed. "It wasn't legal."

Jiang Chen froze.

"You were framed. The evidence was fabricated. The judge was paid." She started laughing, but it was a different laugh now. Triumphant. "They killed you for someone else's crime."

"How do you—"

"When you possess someone, you see everything." She released him, stepped back. "I can prove it. I can show you who really did it. I can give you names."

Jiang Chen's hands were shaking. The staff felt heavy. "You're lying."

"I'm a con woman. Lying is what I do." She smiled. "But I'm not lying about this. I've been possessing people for seventeen years. I've seen every secret, every crime, every hidden truth. There are people in your government who kill for less than what they took from you."

"Tell me the names."

"Take me back to Diyu first. I'll tell the judges everything. They'll have to re-examine your case. You'll get justice."

Justice.

The word felt foreign. He'd been executed three weeks ago. He'd accepted his death, made peace with it, moved on. And now this woman was telling him it was all a lie.

"Think about it," she said. "Someone powerful enough to frame you for murder. Someone connected enough to corrupt a judge. Someone who wanted you dead badly enough to pay for it." She tilted her head. "Who did you offend, Jiang Chen?"

He didn't know. He'd been a nobody. A low-level accountant who got caught up with the wrong people. He'd taken a bribe, yes. He'd looked the other way a few times. But murder? Never.

"The names," he said. "Give them to me."

"Not yet. Take me to Diyu. Let me speak to the judges. Then you'll know." She extended her hand. "Deal?"

Jiang Chen looked at her hand. It was the hand of a woman who'd killed two soul reapers, conned dozens of families, stolen a body and a life that wasn't hers. She was dangerous. She was lying. She was using him.

But she was also his only lead.

He took her hand.

"Fine. But if you try anything—"

She didn't let him finish. Her hand clamped around his, and her soul surged forward, trying to possess him again. He felt her claws in his mind, digging, searching.

"I know what you're doing," she hissed. "You're going to drag me back and let the judges wipe my memory. You'll never get the truth."

"Let go of my mind."

"Let go of your revenge."

He grabbed his staff and jammed it into her chest. Black light exploded. She screamed
